plainMeaning: I confess any harm I caused by stepping on tiny living things — creatures, seeds, sprouts, dew, ant-hills, moss, water, mud, or spider-webs — as I walked.
recitation: pāṇak-kamaṇe, bīyak-kamaṇe, hariyak-kamaṇe, osā, uttiṅga, paṇaga, daga, maṭṭī, makkaḍā-saṁtāṇā saṁkamaṇe

wordByWord.etymology: pāṇa (Skt. prāṇa, 'a breathing being') + kkamaṇe, locative of kamaṇa (Skt. kramaṇa, 'stepping, treading') from the root √kram, 'to step/go'.
wordByWord.notes: The locative -e marks the occasion: 'while stepping on …'. The doubled -kk- is regular Prakrit gemination at the compound juncture.

wordByWord.etymology: bīya (Skt. bīja, 'seed') + kkamaṇe ('treading'); seeds count as plant-bodied (vanaspati) life.

wordByWord.gloss: in treading upon green vegetation / sprouts
wordByWord.etymology: hariya (Skt. harita, 'green, fresh growth') + kkamaṇe ('treading'); the freshly sprouted greenery underfoot.
Skt. avaśyāya, 'dew/hoar-frost'; the water-bodied (ap-kāya) life clinging as droplets.
wordByWord.notes: Sometimes glossed as fine surface moisture rather than dew specifically (variant reading).

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit uttiṅga; traditionally the raised earth of ant-hills, hence the minute beings dwelling there.
wordByWord.notes: Variant glosses include a kind of moth or small insect; see preface glossary.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. panaka, 'moss, mildew'; a one-sensed plant-body (vanaspati) easily crushed underfoot.
wordByWord.notes: Already defined in the preface glossary; given here only for full coverage.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. udaka, 'water' (uda- → daga by Prakrit aphaeresis); standing water as ap-kāya (water-bodied) life.
Skt. mṛttikā, 'earth, clay'; earth-bodied (pṛthvī-kāya) life, including the water mingled in it.
makkaḍa (Skt. markaṭa, here 'spider') + saṁtāṇa (Skt. santāna, 'web, continuous thread'); the gossamer one may break while passing.
wordByWord.notes: Already defined in the preface glossary; included for token coverage.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. saṅkramaṇa, 'crossing, passing over', from sam-√kram; locative -e, the summary verb clamping the whole list: 'in crossing [all these]'.
wordByWord.notes: Echoes the three -kkamaṇe heads, framing items 4–9 (osā … makkaḍā-saṁtāṇā) under one act of 'crossing'.

literalTranslation: In treading on living creatures, in treading on seeds, in treading on green sprouts; dew, ant-hills, moss, water, mud, and spider-webs — in crossing over [them].
idiomaticTranslation: For trampling on tiny creatures, on seeds, on tender greenery; for crossing over dew, ant-hills, moss, standing water, mud, and the threads of spiders' webs — [for all this I now atone].

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Having narrowed the fault to 'going and coming' in the previous verse, this verse opens the lens onto exactly what lies underfoot. Grammatically it falls into two movements. First, three parallel -kkamaṇe ('treading') compounds name the most obvious victims of a footstep: pāṇa (small creatures), bīya (seeds), and hariya (fresh greenery) — already an ascent from mobile life to dormant plant-life to growing plant-life. Then a bare list of six — osā (dew), uttiṅga (ant-hills), paṇaga (moss), daga (water), maṭṭī (mud), makkaḍā-saṁtāṇā (spider-webs) — is gathered up by a single summarizing locative, saṁkamaṇe ('in crossing over'). The shift from 'treading' to 'crossing' is deliberate: one does not merely step on dew or a cobweb, one passes through and disturbs it. Notice that the list is not random clutter but a deliberate inventory of the four sthāvara (immobile) body-types underfoot: earth (maṭṭī, uttiṅga), water (daga, osā), and plant (bīya, hariya, paṇaga), with the spider's thread standing for the dwellings of mobile creatures. The verse thus refuses to let the reciter imagine 'empty ground': every surface is populated. Several terms carry traditional variants (osā as dew or fine moisture; uttiṅga as ant-hill or moth), and the reciter confesses under whichever reading applies.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The doctrinal weight of this verse is the Jain teaching of ekendriya (one-sensed) sthāvara life. Dew and standing water are ap-kāya (water-bodied) souls; mud and the earth of ant-hills are pṛthvī-kāya (earth-bodied) souls; seeds, greenery, and moss are vanaspati-kāya (plant-bodied) souls. Because all four immobile body-classes hold genuine jīvas possessing the sense of touch, no patch of ground is doctrinally neutral and no footstep is innocent. This is precisely why īryā-samiti (carefulness of movement) is an obligation and not mere fastidiousness: harm in motion is structurally unavoidable, which is why this very confession must be repeated. The verse operationalizes ahiṁsā by making the practitioner enumerate, concretely, the unseen life he routinely crushes.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: A modern reciter is invited to look down with new eyes: the dew on morning grass, the mud after rain, the cobweb in a doorway, the moss on a step are not 'nothing' but inhabited. The verse trains an attentive, soft-footed mindfulness — walking deliberately, choosing one's path, and, where harm was unavoidable, naming it honestly rather than ignoring it.

crossReferences.text: Daśavaikālika-sūtra 4 (Ṣaḍ-jīvanikāya — the six classes of living bodies, incl. pṛthvī, ap, and vanaspati kāya)

sources.consultedFor: Sanskrit roots and source-forms: kramaṇa/√kram, bīja, harita, udaka, mṛttikā, panaka, santāna, avaśyāya, saṅkramaṇa

sources.title: JainQQ Jain eLibrary — Āvaśyaka / Pratikramaṇa sūtra texts and translations
sources.consultedFor: the standard Iriyāvahiyaṁ object-litany and its received English glosses (creatures, seeds, greenery, dew, ant-hills, moss, water, mud, spider-webs)

sources.title: Pāia-sadda-mahaṇṇavo (Prakrit-Sanskrit-Hindi dictionary), H. T. Sheth
sources.consultedFor: Prakrit lexical forms pāṇa, paṇaga, uttiṅga, daga, makkaḍa, saṁtāṇa and their attested glosses

notes: Several object-terms carry traditional variant glosses honored in this tradition's knowledge base: osā = dew (or fine surface moisture); uttiṅga = ant-hill / small ground-insect (or, in some glosses, a moth); paṇaga = moss / mildew / fungus; daga = udaka (water); maṭṭī = mud / clay. 'Treading' (-kkamaṇe) heads the first three items, while the remaining six are clamped by the single summarizing 'crossing over' (saṁkamaṇe); the syntax is a litany, not a finite sentence, so '[I atone]' is supplied from the sūtra's opening resolve. Source URLs point to stable scholarly portals; the exact archive.org copy of Sheth's dictionary may vary by upload — verify the specific scan before publication.
